data.title: Navigating Earnings Season After A Tumultuous Year For Tech
data.description:
By Jim Iuorio

The Nasdaq 100 has seen a significant surge this year, driven by the success of major technology firms and their advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and innovation. However, with each earnings season brings its own set of challenges, and investors must be prepared to navigate potential market uncertainty.

Big Tech's Dominance

In the first half of 2025, the Nasdaq 100, which is often seen as a gauge of the technology sector's health, has experienced a tumultuous journey. As of July 3, 2025, the Nasdaq 100 was up nearly 9%, but behind this figure lies a more complex story of market resilience and rapid shifts in sentiment.

The year began with significant downward pressure on the index. From late February to April 8, the Nasdaq 100 lost more than 25% of its value amid global trade concerns. However, April 8 marked a turning point, with the index subsequently rising nearly 40% to reach new all-time highs.

This rebound is intriguing and can be attributed to several potential factors. The market low on April 8 seemed to be an emotional response to the uncertainty surrounding tariff policies. However, by mid-April, it became apparent that the actual impact of these policies was less severe than initially expected, and the timing of their effects had been delayed.

Contrary to widespread analyst warnings that tariffs could exacerbate inflation, the Consumer Price Index (CPI) actually decreased from 2.85% in the spring to 2.4% in May. The slight increase to 2.7% in June was largely attributed to the rise in crude oil prices following the conflict between Israel and Iran.

However, the broader decline in inflation has surprised many, including Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell, who had cautioned about a potential inflationary rebound. Despite these concerns, financial markets, such as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100, are currently at all-time highs.

The 90-day tariff pause, initially set to end on July 9, 2025, was extended to August 1, 2025. The first six months of 2025 have shown that significant changes in macroeconomic government policy can lead to market disruptions and volatility. The passage of the "Big Beautiful Bill" may be one reason for the current market buoyancy, as equity markets tend to thrive on certainty, and the bill's passage has provided some.

The Next Six Months: Key Themes to Watch

Two major themes look to dominate the technology landscape. The most prominent is AI – a continuous tailwind that has propelled Nvidia (NVDA) to become the largest market cap in the Nasdaq 100. This trend shows no signs of slowing down, with recent headlines such as Meta's (META) investment in Scale AI receiving positive investor approval.

Another emerging narrative is energy. Both AI and quantum computing require substantial energy resources. To address this, several major tech companies are partnering with nuclear power providers. For example, Microsoft (MSFT) is developing plans to reactivate the Three Mile Island nuclear facility to meet its energy demands. These developments also appear to be a tailwind for the share prices of the various tech companies.

Navigating Earnings Season Volatility

As earnings season approaches, it's worth noting that the top ten companies in the Nasdaq 100 currently constitute approximately 50% of the total index by market capitalization. Six of these will report earnings in the last two weeks of July.

While some market observers may be concerned about the index's concentrated nature and its vulnerability to volatility from potential earnings surprises, this concentration also presents a hedging opportunity without requiring individual stock positions.

Choosing the right index is essential for all traders and investors. CME Group offers a wide range of futures and options contracts, including the Micro E-mini Nasdaq-100 futures, which provide significant capital efficiency with a notional value of about $45,809 and an initial margin requirement of just 5% to 10%.

In 2025, these futures hit a record average daily volume of 1.8 million contracts, accounting for 55% of the entire Micro E-mini futures suite. This surge in volume highlights their critical role in safeguarding portfolios against earnings volatility and broader market movements, making them a valuable tool for traders of every size.

Similarly, CME Group’s Spot-Quoted Nasdaq-100 futures have a notional value of about $2,300 and a margin requirement that is also in the 5% to 10% range. The lower margin requirements make it easier for retail investors to manage their risk without tying up large amounts of capital. This flexibility is especially important during earnings season, when market movements can be unpredictable and rapid.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: U.S. Stocks Soar To New Highs As Fears Of Bubble Bursting Rise
data.description:
The S&P 500 hit multiple record highs over the past month, rallying against US policy uncertainty, but experts warn that the market bubble could be reaching bursting point.

On June 27, the S&P 500 settled at a record high for the first time in 128 days, hitting record highs another 11 times since then and posting a new record every day in the week of July 20. This record-breaking rally in US stocks has taken place amid a groundswell of policy uncertainty, including higher tariffs and stricter immigration enforcement, volatile geopolitics, inflation that refuses to cool, and a job market showing signs of strain.

As stocks continue to rally in spite of it all, investors fear that a bubble has formed, with some bracing for it to burst soon, economists and market strategists said.

"With the current bubble, there is more confirming evidence of the existence of a bubble than you can shake a stick at," said Steve H. Hanke, a professor of applied economics at Johns Hopkins University.

"But the problem with bubbles is that it is next to impossible to predict when they will pop or whether the air will simply be released slowly as markets adjust to more realistic valuations."

Since falling to an April trough over worries about President Donald Trump's push to raise tariffs to historically high levels, the S&P 500 has surged close to 30%. The sentiment that drives the market has shifted quickly to bullish from bearish and now appears to be close to a speculative frenzy as new headlines of war, torpid economic data, and middling consumer confidence are met with increasing stock gains, Hanke said.

"No matter what the news, the market just goes up," Hanke said.

Michael O'Rourke, Chief Market Strategist at JonesTrading, said the S&P 500 is now trading at a record 188% of US GDP, well above the 177% peak in late 2021 and the 125% peak during the dot-com bubble in 2000.

The recent resurgence of meme stock trading — where retail investors target heavily shorted, low-priced stocks — is an argument that the market has reached bubble status, O'Rourke said. Shares of GoPro Inc. (GPRO), Kohl's Corp. (KSS) and Krispy Kreme Inc. (DNUT) saw steep and sudden price increases in July, similar to the sudden rallies GameStop Corp. (GME) and AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc. (AMC) saw in 2021, at the height of the meme stock craze.

"We are not seeing investment based on earnings power, it is speculation counting on greater fools," O'Rourke said. "The stock market has become a caricature of investing."

AI narrative

Every market bubble in modern history has been backed by a narrative, such as the financial viability of the internet, which bolstered the dot-com bubble, and the sweeping pull of earnings potential in real estate, which led to the 2008–2009 financial crisis, said Tyler Richey, co-editor of Sevens Report Research.

The bubble-inflating theme now is likely the promise of AI technology, as tech stocks have seen outsize gains in stock prices and valuations. Nvidia Corp. (NVDA), a proxy for the health of the AI trade, saw its market capitalization cross $4 trillion in July. Nvidia's market cap increased $1.933 trillion since the market bottomed in April.

Still, the robust health of Nvidia is likely an extreme outlier, Richey believes, as the broader-based semiconductor index — which includes other major AI chipmakers such as Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD), Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM), Micron Technology Inc. (MU), Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL) — remains below both its July 2024 high and the S&P 500.

Richey compared the relative performance of the semiconductor index over the past 12 months to the Road Runner in the "Looney Tunes" cartoon series and the S&P 500 to the Wile E. Coyote character. The semiconductor index has now stopped abruptly at the edge of a cliff, while the S&P 500 has gone screeching off the side "to face the laws of gravity," Richey said.

"A downward force that the broader stock market could very well be on the brink of facing itself," Richey added.

Multidecade extremes in the relative strength of equity indexes, imbalances in the technical outlook for market sectors and a downside divergence in investor sentiment reading despite market highs are signs that the equities bubble could burst soon, according to Richey.

No disconnect

Still, the rally could have more room to run.

"Current S&P 500 valuations and price levels do not seem disconnected from today's economic conditions, corporate earnings or consumer strength," said Adam Hetts, global head of multi-asset and portfolio manager at Janus Henderson. "Although high valuations warrant increased vigilance in security selection, this market continues to be broadly justified by fundamentals."

The market appears content to continue to break new highs despite the headwinds of US economic policy and other global events.

"The market has shown remarkable resilience this year," Hetts said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Silver Bull Market Likely To Continue For 4 Reasons
data.description:
When I analyze an investment to determine whether it is likely to be in a bull or bear trend market going forward, I use what I call the “four steps to bull and bear profits”. These four steps are as follows:

  1. estimating long-term returns
  2. forecasting the economy
  3. identifying potential trend changes
  4. identifying the current trend

This is a more comprehensive approach to investment analysis than many investors perform, as they usually only focus on one or two of these four key steps.

In this article, I will use this analysis to determine whether the silver bull market is likely to continue, as I argued a year ago in this Seeking Alpha article, or whether it will soon start a major bear market. I performed similar analysis on gold in this recent Seeking Alpha article.

In my opinion, the weight of the evidence is that the silver bull market is likely to continue for the foreseeable future.

1. Estimating Long-Term Returns

This chart shows the price of the Dow Jones Industrial Average (“DJIA”) versus silver since 1915. As the chart shows, the DJIA has outperformed or underperformed silver for long periods of time. For example, the DJIA underperformed silver during the difficult decades for stocks of the 1930s, 1970s, and 2000s. And while the DJIA has underperformed silver over the past 24 years, it has outperformed over the past 14 years The red trend lines show when the DJIA outperformance broke historically. Note that it has also broken the latest red trend line, which is a bearish sign for the DJIA but a bullish sign for silver.

What are the fundamental reasons that stocks outperform and underperform silver for long periods of time? Two key reasons: valuation and fundamental economic and psychological factors.

When stocks become very expensive and fundamental economic and psychological factors turn more risky and uncertain, stocks typically underperform silver for long periods of time. Conversely, when stocks become very inexpensive and fundamental economic and psychological factors turn less risky and more certain, the stocks typically outperform silver for long periods of time.

This next chart shows the valuation metric that most accurately forecasts the returns of the S&P 500 over the coming 12 years, according to the research of fund manager John Hussman: non financial market capitalization / non financial gross value added including estimated foreign revenues, which is similar to Warren Buffett’s preferred stock market valuation metric of stock market capitalization / GDP.

Currently, the US stock market is near the highest valuation levels in history, as this chart shows.

As a result, based on nearly a century of history, the S&P 500 is likely to generate negative 6% annual total returns over the coming 12 years, as shown here.

Since silver does not generate income, I believe the best method to get a sense of the valuation of silver is by comparing the price of silver to the overall economy, stocks, and gold over time. If silver is towards the upper end of its historical valuation range relative to those variables, then it is likely expensive. Conversely, if it is towards the lower end of its historical relative valuation range, it is likely inexpensive. Currently, silver appears to be very inexpensive on a relative basis, as we show below.

This chart shows the ratio of silver to GDP since 1980. If silver were to return to its 2011 valuation relative to GDP, it would have about 120% upside from current levels. If it were to return to its 1980 valuation relative to GDP, it would have about 560% upside.

This chart shows the ratio of silver to the S&P 500 since 1980. If silver were to return to its 2011 valuation relative to the S&P 500, it would have about 550% upside from current levels. If it were to return to its 1980 valuation relative to the S&P 500, it would have about 7500% upside.

This chart shows the ratio of silver to gold since 1980. If silver were to return to its 2011 valuation relative to gold, it would have about 175% upside from current levels. If it were to return to its 1980 valuation relative to gold, it would have about 370% upside. if silver were simply to return to its average price relative to gold since 1980 (1/60th), silver would reach about $55, which is over 40% upside.

Based on these measures, silver is clearly much cheaper than it was at its 1980 peak and is even cheaper than it was at its 2011 high. Thus, on a valuation and long-term return basis, the silver bull market appears likely to continue.

2. Forecasting The Economy

In addition to valuation, I believe the current economic and psychological environment is becoming more bullish for silver.

The 1930s outperformance of silver was aided by the disaster of the Great Depression and the rise of fascism and increasing government interventions in the economy, which ultimately led to World War II. The 1970s outperformance of silver was aided by “stagflationary” recessions, the oil crisis in the Middle East and political conflict relating to the Vietnam War, Watergate and the Iranian hostage crisis. The 2000s outperformance of silver was aided by the tech bust, housing bust, Great Recession, 9/11 and the “war on terror”.

Now we have increasing economic and political uncertainty once again, including with the new Trump presidency and his high tariff and budget policies. The US Federal budget deficit is particularly concerning. It has worsened materially over the past 10 years and the deficit is now about 6% of GDP. The Congressional Budget Office estimates the deficit will rise to 7.3% of GDP by 2055 and public debt to GDP will rise from 100% now to 156% by 2055.

Inflation remains stubbornly about the Fed’s 2% target, which is why the Fed is not cutting interest rates, This is despite The Conference Board’s US Leading Economic Index (LEI) falling for over three years, much longer than the decline that led to Great Recession of 2008-2009, as shown here. A “stagflationary” economy is bullish for silver.

3. Identifying Potential Trend Changes

The best sign of a potential change in the trend of an investment is investor sentiment. When investors are very bullish on an asset — as they generally are now about stocks — that is contrarian bearish and it suggests a bear market is likely coming at some point. Conversely, when investors are very bearish on an asset, that is contrarian bullish and it suggests a bull market is likely coming at some point.

Remarkably, despite the outperformance of silver versus stocks over the past 24 years and the past year, in particular, the market share of silver ETFs remains relatively low and much lower than during the silver bull market of the 2000s, as the chart below shows. This shows investor sentiment towards silver remains surprising lukewarm, which suggests the bull market is not about to end soon.

4. Identifying The Current Trend

Now let’s look at the technical indicators of silver’s price trend. The chart below shows the monthly silver price (top clip) since 1995. Note that silver broke above the trend line that contained prices from 2011 to early 2024, similar to the breakout that occurred in 2003. This suggests more to go in the silver bull market. Also, the price relative to the S&P 500 (second clip) shows it has recently started to outperform the S&P 500 after about a decade of inline performance. In addition, long-term monthly momentum is positive (third clip), as is quarterly momentum (fourth clip), based on the PPO indicator. The primary concern is silver is “long-term overbought”, based on the monthly RSI (fifth clip) and monthly Stochastics (sixth clip). However, silver was also long-term overbought many times during its bull market run in the 2000s, so I believe this is less important than the bullish trend indicators noted above.

This last chart shows the monthly silver price relative to the S&P 500 (top clip) since 1995. The message is similar to the absolute price chart shown above. This year, silver has broken above the trend line that contained prices in recent years. Relative long-term monthly momentum is also positive (third clip), as is quarterly momentum (fourth clip). And on a relative basis, silver does not appear “long-term overbought”.

These technical indicators suggest the bull market trend of silver is likely to remain your friend for a while longer.

Conclusion

Based on our “four steps to bull and bear profits” analysis, the silver bull market looks like it has more room to run, particularly relative to stocks. This is based on its attractive valuation, favorable economic and fundamental factors, contrarian bullish sentiment indicators and bullish trend indicators. Based on my analysis, I believe silver is likely to outshine stocks as one of the best assets in the coming years, along with gold.

I believe one of the easiest and best ways to profit from the silver bull market is to buy the iShares Silver Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:SLV). It invests in physical silver, as do similar silver ETFs like abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF (SIVR) and Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV). SLV's expense ratio is 0.50%. That is below PSLV's 0.58% ratio, but above SIVR's 0.30% expense ratio. But at $18.5 billion in AUM, SLV is more than eight times larger than SIVR, so it has much higher liquidity and trading volume, which makes it easier to trade in and out of.

data.title: July FOMC Meeting: Ruling Nothing Out
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Managing Director, Chief Strategist

At its July meeting, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) decided to keep its benchmark policy rate unchanged at 4.25%-4.50% as expected. However, this was not a unanimous decision. For the first time since 1993, there were two dissents, with Fed Governors Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman voting to cut policy rates by 25 basis points.

Even so, the press conference provided no clear policy direction. Chair Powell did not offer any clues or guidance around the September FOMC meeting, although his broader comments leaned marginally more hawkish than the market had anticipated. With two employment reports and two inflation prints to be released ahead of the next meeting, the outlook for Fed cuts remains wide open.

Current policy backdrop

As well as contending with White House criticism of Chair Powell and the decision to keep policy rates on hold over recent months, the Fed has also had to confront an uncertain trade policy backdrop and conflicting economic signals.

Survey data continue to suggest consumer weakness, while economic activity data remained broadly robust. In the labor market, hirings are low, but so too are firings, while declining labor supply because of tighter immigration policy is likely to make employment data increasingly difficult to decipher in the coming months.

Inflation has been trending lower, but is beginning to show signs of tariff pass-through to core goods prices. Against this backdrop, policy decisions are even less straightforward than usual, and so it is unsurprising that there are conflicting views within the committee.

Press conference - key observations

Chair Powell delivered several important insights about how the Fed is currently viewing the economic backdrop.

Tariff pass-through to inflation: Powell noted that, while it is still early days, tariffs are expected to be passed through to consumers. The Fed’s base case is that the tariff-induced boost to inflation will likely prove short-lived, reflecting a one-time shift in the price level - but, with so many uncertainties still left to be resolved, it is appropriate to guard against inflation risk.

Tariff uncertainty: Recent news flow has cleared up some of the tariff questions, but tariff discussions likely still have much further to go - so tariff uncertainty remains elevated.

Labor market supply dynamics: Powell described the labor market as robust and that the full-employment target is currently being met. He acknowledged that labor demand is cooling and that there are downside risks but, as labor supply has also decreased due to declining immigration flows, the labor market is in balance.

Current policy stance: The Fed funds rate is sitting at “modestly restrictive” levels. Powell noted that of their two targets in their dual mandate, inflation is further away from goal than the labor market is. If the two goals move closer into balance, then policy should move to a more neutral setting.

Policy outlook

Chair Powell gave away very little during Wednesday’s press conference. This shouldn’t be a surprise - there is currently too much uncertainty for the Fed to signal a clear policy direction for September.

However, FOMC Governor Waller has more conviction than Powell (and us) about the outlook and has adopted a very dovish tone. His concern that labor market demand is cooling rapidly is gaining traction, both within the committee (as reflected in the dissent from Governor Bowman) and among analysts.

It is not inconceivable that he will gain more support over the coming weeks, particularly if the upcoming jobs and inflation reports are weak, suggesting that the odds of a September rate cut are fair.

Our own view has been that the robustness of the economy, alongside expectations for a tariff-induced boost to inflation, not to mention persistent government policy uncertainty, mean that the Fed should remain on hold until later this year. But, with the drumbeat apparently growing louder within the Fed, an earlier rate cut is a clear possibility.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
